transcript.transcript: Okay, now, I lost your picture, Margaret, but I can see you there. Me too. Now I have you back. Okay. Oh, there you are. All right. Okay, so this is the, uh, America 250 oral history project through the Idaho Historical Society. Yes. So this is the introduction. Uh, my name is Nancy Inaba. I am part of a committee, um, from Idaho Watercolor Society. Uh, doing the history of our 50 year anniversary. Uh, today's date is November 25th, and I am with Margaret Conrad, also known as Peggy, to talk about, um, her history with watercolor and Idaho Watercolor Society. Citing the oral history, is conducted with the support of Idaho Historical Society as part of the America 250 in Idaho. Commemoration. The Oral History Project, of which this interview is a part, supports the commemorations mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution, equality, liberty, and justice through meaningful community engagement and recognize, appreciate, and commemorate Idaho's journey in America's legacy. Margaret Conrad. First, let me thank you for your time today. This interview will allow you to reflect on and share your memories and experiences about the focus of the interview. Through these questions, I hope to gain insight into outcomes of Idaho's role. I mean, Idaho Watercolor Society's role with artists and as an art organization. I'll begin with some general questions and then get more specific questions. Um, so before we jump into the questions, do you give permission to record this interview? And can I have you state and spell your first and last name? Of course I do give permission. And my first name is Margaret m a r g a r e t. My last name is Conrad. C o n r a d. Okay. And do you like to be called Margaret or Peggy? Most people call me Peggy. Okay. All right. Um, so let's see. Can you know I've never met you before? And can you tell me something about yourself? Well, uh, yes. I first, uh, joined IWS in 2004, I believe. And, uh, I had returned to Moscow, Idaho, having lived away for some 30 probably years, uh, in the Chicago area and, uh, had painted there, of course. And, um, returned here and joined the IWS. Mhm. Um, and is your family from Idaho originally? Yes. Actually, I was born here in Moscow. My mother was born in Moscow. Um, my father was not, but lived here his whole adult life. Mhm. Yeah. I was up in that, um, Coeur d'Alene area and I met a, a man who family had. He was older. His family had been in Idaho for five generations. Um, well, actually, I have a number of generations here, um, myself, you know, the cemetery here, I go back, my great grandparents and so on are buried there, and, so actually I have a number of generations here myself. Mhm. And where did they come from. Where did they migrate from. Uh, originally on my mother's side, uh, from Missouri, from my father's side, North Carolina. That was a big change. Yes. A long time ago. Yeah. I oftentimes think how hard life was for them without, uh, Polipropolene you know, without fleece to wear. Oh, my gosh. Yes. We're realizing that today here, it's quite cold. Yes. The temperature just dropped here recently, too. Um, but, yeah, I go between hot and cold. You'll see me do a costume changes,three times. Um, so you were you working in in Chicago? Yes, I was, I was a teacher there. um, and taught in the same school district for 30 years. So. Um, yes. So you came back to Idaho kind of as your retirement home? Yes I did. My mother had died a few years earlier, and I had bought her house, not really knowing whether I would ever live here or not. Somehow I just bought it from the estate. I didn't, I guess, want to see it go to someone else. So I'm still living there in this house and have found, uh, returning. I wasn't sure, having lived near a city for quite some time, I wasn't sure if Moscow was going to be the place, you know, but, uh, actually, it's been a wonderful place. Oh, you have the university there? Any time there's a university, it makes any small town a unique town. Yeah, it does. And of course, we have WSU, you know, six miles away. So. Right. Yes, we have that also. Yeah. There's great opportunities being close to, uh, large institutions. Very much so. Yes. And since I had gone to school here and I graduated from the University of Idaho, um, so I still had friends in the area on account of that. And had, you know, maintained friendships over time. So it wasn't like I was moving to a new place at all. In fact, uh, always returning here was, um, brought a lot of peace to me somehow. And it used to be true. Yeah. Did you find that your paintings changed when you moved from the Midwest out to Moscow? Uh, probably. I think, um, you know, I started doing some different things with my painting than I had done before? Um, yes, probably. For one thing, you know, I mean, a lot of times you're painting landscape of some kind or other. And this is quite different here as compared to the Chicago area. So yes, I think that did it did. Yeah. I think it's kind of fascinating the way that our environment influences what inspires us. Yes, very much so. Yeah. Um let's see. Let's see. So have you always painted? I always have done art. Mhm. Um, however, uh, I actually, um, I didn't really start with watercolor so much until I was probably, I would say 50 ish. Something like that is when I really got started with watercolor. Um, uh, probably, you know, because of the university here, uh, that probably has influenced Moscow as a town. But there's quite a bit of art going on here. Um, another group yet, uh, Palouse women artists, for instance, just very active. So, um, we have, um, a lot of people in the area who, uh, have something to do with art one way or the other. Right. lines.line: # Introduction and Permissions

> The interview begins with technical difficulties as the participants connect virtually. Nancy introduces herself and the America 250 oral history project, explaining its purpose and the involvement of the Idaho Watercolor Society. She outlines the goals of the interview and requests permission to record, setting a formal and respectful tone for the conversation.

metadata.title: Introduction and Permissions
metadata.synopsis: The interview begins with technical difficulties as the participants connect virtually. Nancy introduces herself and the America 250 oral history project, explaining its purpose and the involvement of the Idaho Watercolor Society. She outlines the goals of the interview and requests permission to record, setting a formal and respectful tone for the conversation.

lines.line: # Personal Background and Connection to Idaho

> Margaret provides her full name and preferred nickname, Peggy, and shares her personal history. She discusses her family's deep roots in Idaho, including multiple generations buried in the local cemetery, and describes her return to Moscow after decades in Chicago. The conversation touches on her family's migration from Missouri and North Carolina, and her decision to retire in her mother's house, highlighting her strong ties to the region.

metadata.title: Personal Background and Connection to Idaho
metadata.synopsis: Margaret provides her full name and preferred nickname, Peggy, and shares her personal history. She discusses her family's deep roots in Idaho, including multiple generations buried in the local cemetery, and describes her return to Moscow after decades in Chicago. The conversation touches on her family's migration from Missouri and North Carolina, and her decision to retire in her mother's house, highlighting her strong ties to the region.

lines.line: # Artistic Journey and Watercolor Experience

> Margaret reflects on how her artistic style evolved after moving from Chicago to Moscow, noting the influence of the local landscape. She shares that she has always been involved in art but only began focusing on watercolor in her fifties, benefiting from workshops and classes in the Chicago area. The discussion emphasizes the impact of environment and education on her artistic development.

metadata.title: Artistic Journey and Watercolor Experience
metadata.synopsis: Margaret reflects on how her artistic style evolved after moving from Chicago to Moscow, noting the influence of the local landscape. She shares that she has always been involved in art but only began focusing on watercolor in her fifties, benefiting from workshops and classes in the Chicago area. The discussion emphasizes the impact of environment and education on her artistic development.

lines.line: # Art Scene in Northern Idaho

> The conversation explores the vibrant art community in northern Idaho, including the Palouse Watercolor Society and other local groups. Margaret describes the geographic spread of members, the influence of nearby universities, and the presence of active organizations such as Palouse Women Artists. She highlights the challenges and opportunities of creating art in a region with significant distances between communities.

metadata.title: Art Scene in Northern Idaho
metadata.synopsis: The conversation explores the vibrant art community in northern Idaho, including the Palouse Watercolor Society and other local groups. Margaret describes the geographic spread of members, the influence of nearby universities, and the presence of active organizations such as Palouse Women Artists. She highlights the challenges and opportunities of creating art in a region with significant distances between communities.

lines.line: # Regional Organization and Volunteer Challenges

> Margaret explains the historical structure of the Idaho Watercolor Society, noting the consolidation of regions and the ongoing difficulty in finding volunteers for leadership roles. She recounts her own experience as a regional representative, the impact of losing key volunteers, and the challenges of maintaining engagement across large distances. The discussion underscores the importance and difficulty of sustaining volunteer-driven organizations.

metadata.title: Regional Organization and Volunteer Challenges
metadata.synopsis: Margaret explains the historical structure of the Idaho Watercolor Society, noting the consolidation of regions and the ongoing difficulty in finding volunteers for leadership roles. She recounts her own experience as a regional representative, the impact of losing key volunteers, and the challenges of maintaining engagement across large distances. The discussion underscores the importance and difficulty of sustaining volunteer-driven organizations.

metadata.title: COVID-19, Loss, and Organizational Challenges
metadata.synopsis: Margaret recounts a tumultuous period marked by the COVID-19 pandemic, the death of a key member, and a truck accident that destroyed paintings. She describes the community's response, including memorial awards and the printing of lost artworks, as well as difficulties in securing venues for exhibitions. These events illustrate the resilience and adaptability of the organization during times of crisis. This section also covered what it was like for members of the organization during the COVID-19.

lines.line: # Local and Regional Art Shows

> Margaret discusses the frequency and variety of local art exhibitions, including collaborations with the Spokane Watercolor Society and the Palouse Watercolor Associates. She emphasizes the significance of the traveling show, which sets a high standard for artists statewide and provides valuable opportunities for exposure and inspiration. The conversation highlights the importance of regional and statewide exhibitions for artistic growth.

metadata.title: Local and Regional Art Shows
metadata.synopsis: Margaret discusses the frequency and variety of local art exhibitions, including collaborations with the Spokane Watercolor Society and the Palouse Watercolor Associates. She emphasizes the significance of the traveling show, which sets a high standard for artists statewide and provides valuable opportunities for exposure and inspiration. The conversation highlights the importance of regional and statewide exhibitions for artistic growth.

lines.line: # Artistic Passion, Practice, and Personal Growth

> Margaret shares her commitment to daily artistic practice, including maintaining a diary to track accomplishments and viewing watercolor as a central part of her life. She describes the ongoing process of learning, experimenting, and finding fulfillment in art, both for herself and for other passionate artists in the northern region. The discussion emphasizes the personal and communal value of artistic dedication.

lines.line: # Watercolor and Younger Generations

> The conversation turns to the appeal of watercolor among younger artists, noting the predominance of older members in local groups. Margaret observes changes in watercolor styles and expectations over time, the influence of technology on art consumption, and the challenges of engaging younger generations. She reflects on the evolution of artistic standards and the search for individuality in contemporary watercolor.

lines.line: # Reflections on Artistic Achievement and Organizational History

> Margaret recalls significant personal achievements, such as winning second place in a state juried show, and expresses gratitude for the opportunities provided by the Idaho Watercolor Society. The conversation includes plans for upcoming events, recognition of volunteer efforts, and reflections on the organization's history and the dedication required to sustain it. The discussion highlights the sense of community and accomplishment among members.

> The interview concludes with expressions of gratitude and logistical details about the recording's archival location. The participants exchange farewells and discuss minor technical issues, ending the conversation on a positive and appreciative note.

children.text: # Introduction and Permissions

> The interview begins with technical difficulties as the participants connect virtually. Nancy introduces herself and the America 250 oral history project, explaining its purpose and the involvement of the Idaho Watercolor Society. She outlines the goals of the interview and requests permission to record, setting a formal and respectful tone for the conversation.
# Personal Background and Connection to Idaho

> Margaret provides her full name and preferred nickname, Peggy, and shares her personal history. She discusses her family's deep roots in Idaho, including multiple generations buried in the local cemetery, and describes her return to Moscow after decades in Chicago. The conversation touches on her family's migration from Missouri and North Carolina, and her decision to retire in her mother's house, highlighting her strong ties to the region.
# Artistic Journey and Watercolor Experience

> Margaret reflects on how her artistic style evolved after moving from Chicago to Moscow, noting the influence of the local landscape. She shares that she has always been involved in art but only began focusing on watercolor in her fifties, benefiting from workshops and classes in the Chicago area. The discussion emphasizes the impact of environment and education on her artistic development.
# Art Scene in Northern Idaho

> The conversation explores the vibrant art community in northern Idaho, including the Palouse Watercolor Society and other local groups. Margaret describes the geographic spread of members, the influence of nearby universities, and the presence of active organizations such as Palouse Women Artists. She highlights the challenges and opportunities of creating art in a region with significant distances between communities.
# Regional Organization and Volunteer Challenges

> Margaret explains the historical structure of the Idaho Watercolor Society, noting the consolidation of regions and the ongoing difficulty in finding volunteers for leadership roles. She recounts her own experience as a regional representative, the impact of losing key volunteers, and the challenges of maintaining engagement across large distances. The discussion underscores the importance and difficulty of sustaining volunteer-driven organizations.
# COVID-19, Loss, and Organizational Challenges

> Margaret recounts a tumultuous period marked by the COVID-19 pandemic, the death of a key member, and a truck accident that destroyed paintings. She describes the community's response, including memorial awards and the printing of lost artworks, as well as difficulties in securing venues for exhibitions. These events illustrate the resilience and adaptability of the organization during times of crisis. This section also covered what it was like for members of the organization during the COVID-19.

story.title: Margaret Conrad interview North region
story.description: Margaret served as Regional Director after Betty Benson, along with Cheryll Root
